name: Ansible Galaxy API
what_it_does: The Ansible Galaxy API provides access to the community hub for Ansible roles and collections. Agents can search for roles and collections, retrieve metadata and version information, fetch dependency manifests, and check download statistics. Galaxy NG (the backend) is also used by Red Hat Automation Hub for certified content.
use_cases: Discovering Ansible roles and collections for a given automation task or platform
Resolving collection version requirements for an Ansible playbook requirements file
Checking for newer versions of installed collections for automated update workflows
Fetching role/collection metadata to assess maintenance status and community health
Building automation platform inventories of available Ansible content
not_for: Executing Ansible playbooks (use Ansible CLI or AWX/Controller API)
Managing Ansible Automation Platform (AAP) job templates or inventories (use AWX API)
Hosting private collections without Red Hat Automation Hub or a self-hosted Galaxy NG
best_when: An agent needs to discover, resolve, or audit Ansible roles and collections as part of infrastructure automation workflow planning.
avoid_when: You need to run playbooks, manage inventories, or work with private certified content (use Red Hat Automation Hub or AWX API instead).

auth.notes: Read operations (search, metadata) are unauthenticated on the public Galaxy. Publishing collections requires authentication via token (passed as Authorization: Token header). Tokens are generated from galaxy.ansible.com user settings. The v3 API (collections) and v1 API (legacy roles) have different authentication patterns and different endpoint structures.

pricing.free_tier_limits: All community content is free to access and publish. No rate limits published.
pricing.paid_tiers: Red Hat Automation Hub (certified content) requires Red Hat subscription
pricing.notes: Ansible Galaxy (galaxy.ansible.com) is a free community service operated by Red Hat. Red Hat Automation Hub with certified content requires a Red Hat Ansible Automation Platform subscription.

performance.rate_limits: No officially published rate limits. The service has historically experienced performance issues during peak usage.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses vary significantly between v1 (legacy roles) and v3 (collections) API versions. v3 returns structured errors; v1 errors are more ad-hoc. Rate limit and server error responses are not well-documented. OpenAPI spec is available for v3 but not v1.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: GET operations are idempotent. Collection publish operations via v3 are idempotent if the same version is re-uploaded (returns 409 on conflict). Legacy role imports via v1 are not idempotent and trigger async import jobs.
known_agent_gotchas: Galaxy has two incompatible API versions: v1 (legacy roles) and v3 (modern collections) with completely different URL structures and response schemas
Role imports are asynchronous — the import API returns a task ID that must be polled for completion
Collection namespaces must be pre-approved; publishing to an unapproved namespace returns a confusing error
Galaxy sometimes experiences significant downtime or degraded performance — agents need robust retry and timeout handling
The `ansible-galaxy` CLI handles authentication and collection resolution logic that is non-trivial to replicate via raw API calls
Legacy roles use GitHub as the source of truth; Galaxy re-imports from GitHub on demand rather than storing tarballs directly
Community role quality varies enormously; agents should check last_modified and download_count as proxy quality signals

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Token authentication has no scope granularity. Community role quality and security is unverified by default — agents using roles in production should verify the role source. Red Hat Automation Hub provides certified content with security scanning, but requires a paid subscription. Galaxy community content has no mandatory security review.

score_rationale: Ansible Galaxy's API is functional but frustrating for agent use. The split between v1 (roles) and v3 (collections) API versions with incompatible schemas requires agents to handle two different patterns. Async role imports, no SLA, historically poor uptime, and minimal documentation make this one of the weaker developer registries for agent integration. The v3 OpenAPI spec helps, but coverage is incomplete. Best used for read-only collection metadata lookups; avoid automated publish workflows.
